name: Table
summary: The fundamental structure for storing data in rows and columns.
description: A table is the most basic building block of a relational database. It organizes data into rows (records) and columns (fields), similar to a spreadsheet. Every piece of data you store in a database lives inside a table. Tables have a defined structure (schema) that specifies what columns exist and what type of data each column can hold.
tips: Always choose meaningful table names that describe the data they hold, using plural nouns (e.g., 'users', 'orders').
Define a PRIMARY KEY for every table to uniquely identify each row.
Keep tables focused on a single entity — if a table has too many unrelated columns, consider splitting it.

name: Temporary Table
summary: A table that exists only for the duration of a session or transaction.
description: A temporary table is a short-lived table that is automatically dropped when the session ends or the connection is closed. It is useful for storing intermediate results during complex queries or batch processing. Temporary tables are only visible to the session that created them, so they won't interfere with other users.
tips: Use temporary tables when you need to store intermediate query results for multi-step data processing.
Temporary tables are session-scoped, so two different users can create temp tables with the same name without conflict.
In PostgreSQL, you can use ON COMMIT DROP to make a temp table last only for the current transaction.

name: Foreign Table
summary: A table that references data stored in an external data source.
description: A foreign table allows you to query data from an external source (another database, a CSV file, a remote server) as if it were a regular table in your database. This is part of the SQL/MED (Management of External Data) standard. Foreign tables are commonly used to integrate data across different systems without copying it.

mysql.notes: MySQL uses the FEDERATED storage engine instead of the SQL/MED standard. It must be explicitly enabled.

mariadb.notes: MariaDB uses the FEDERATED or CONNECT engine for remote table access.

tips: Foreign tables are read-heavy — writing to them may be slow or unsupported depending on the wrapper.
In PostgreSQL, use postgres_fdw for connecting to other PostgreSQL databases, or file_fdw for reading CSV/text files.
Always test query performance on foreign tables, as network latency can make them significantly slower than local tables.

name: Column
summary: A named field in a table that holds a specific type of data.
description: A column defines one attribute of the data stored in a table. For example, a 'users' table might have columns like 'username', 'email', and 'created_at'. Each column has a name and a data type that determines what kind of values it can store (text, numbers, dates, etc.). Columns can also have constraints like NOT NULL or DEFAULT values.

sqlite.notes: SQLite only supports ADD COLUMN. You cannot drop or rename columns in older versions (before 3.35.0).

tips: Use descriptive column names that clearly convey the data they hold (e.g., 'created_at' instead of 'date1').
Always specify NOT NULL for columns that should never be empty to enforce data integrity.
Consider adding DEFAULT values for columns so that inserts don't fail if the column is omitted.

name: Data Type
summary: Defines what kind of value a column can store (text, number, date, etc.).
description: A data type tells the database what kind of value a column holds, such as integers, text, dates, or booleans. Choosing the right data type is important because it affects storage efficiency, query performance, and data validation. Each database vendor has its own set of data types, though many are shared across vendors.

syntax.postgresql:
CREATE TABLE products (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  price NUMERIC(10, 2) NOT NULL,
  in_stock BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
  tags TEXT[],
  metadata JSONB,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
syntax.mysql:
CREATE TABLE products (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  price DECIMAL(10, 2) NOT NULL,
  in_stock TINYINT(1) DEFAULT 1,
  tags JSON,
  metadata JSON,
  created_at DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
syntax.mariadb:
CREATE TABLE products (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  price DECIMAL(10, 2) NOT NULL,
  in_stock TINYINT(1) DEFAULT 1,
  tags JSON,
  metadata JSON,
  created_at DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
syntax.sqlite:
CREATE TABLE products (
  id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  price REAL NOT NULL,
  in_stock INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
  tags TEXT,
  metadata TEXT,
  created_at TEXT DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

sqlite.notes: SQLite uses dynamic typing with type affinities (TEXT, INTEGER, REAL, BLOB, NUMERIC) rather than strict data types.

tips: Use the smallest data type that fits your data — e.g., SMALLINT instead of BIGINT for small numbers, to save storage.
Use VARCHAR with a sensible maximum length instead of TEXT when you want to enforce a size limit.
For monetary values, always use NUMERIC/DECIMAL instead of FLOAT to avoid rounding errors.

name: Generated Column
summary: A column whose value is automatically computed from other columns.
description: A generated column (also called a computed column) is a column whose value is derived from an expression using other columns in the same row. Instead of manually computing and storing a value, the database calculates it for you. Generated columns can be 'stored' (physically saved to disk) or 'virtual' (computed on-the-fly when queried).

postgresql.notes: PostgreSQL only supports STORED generated columns (not VIRTUAL) as of version 16.

tips: Use generated columns to avoid data inconsistency — the database guarantees the computed value is always correct.
Virtual generated columns save disk space since they are computed on read, but stored columns are faster to query.
You cannot directly INSERT or UPDATE a generated column — the database manages its value automatically.

name: Identity Column
summary: A column that auto-generates unique sequential values using the SQL standard syntax.
description: An identity column is the SQL standard way of defining a column that automatically generates unique, sequential values. It is the modern replacement for vendor-specific auto-increment features. Identity columns give you more control than auto-increment, such as specifying start values, increments, and whether the generated value can be overridden.

mysql.notes: MySQL does not support identity columns. Use AUTO_INCREMENT instead.

mariadb.notes: MariaDB does not support identity columns. Use AUTO_INCREMENT or SEQUENCE instead.

sqlite.notes: SQLite does not support identity columns. Use INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT instead.

tips: Prefer GENERATED ALWAYS over GENERATED BY DEFAULT — it prevents accidental manual inserts that could cause conflicts.
In PostgreSQL, identity columns are the recommended way to create auto-incrementing IDs instead of using SERIAL.
You can customize the start value and increment step using sequence options inside the identity definition.

name: Auto Increment
summary: Automatically generates a unique incrementing number for each new row.
description: Auto increment is a column feature that automatically assigns a unique, incrementing integer to each new row inserted into a table. It is most commonly used for primary key columns to ensure every row has a unique identifier without manual input. Each vendor implements this slightly differently, but the concept is the same.

postgresql.notes: PostgreSQL uses SERIAL or IDENTITY syntax instead of AUTO_INCREMENT keyword.

tips: In SQLite, INTEGER PRIMARY KEY already auto-increments. Adding AUTOINCREMENT just prevents ID reuse after deletion.
Never assume auto-increment values are gap-free — deletions and failed inserts can create gaps.
In PostgreSQL, prefer IDENTITY columns over SERIAL for new projects as they follow the SQL standard.

name: Column Comment
summary: A text description attached to a column for documentation purposes.
description: A column comment is a piece of metadata that describes the purpose or content of a column. Comments help other developers (and future you) understand what data a column holds, its format, or any business rules it encodes. Comments are stored in the database catalog and can be viewed using database tools.

syntax.postgresql:
-- Add a comment to an existing column
COMMENT ON COLUMN users.email IS 'Primary email address used for login and notifications';

sqlite.notes: SQLite does not support column comments. Use SQL comments in your schema file instead.

tips: Write comments for columns with non-obvious meanings, coded values, or specific format requirements.
Keep comments concise but informative — focus on what the business meaning is, not just the data type.
In MySQL/MariaDB, you must re-specify the full column definition when modifying a comment.

name: Table Comment
summary: A text description attached to a table for documentation purposes.
description: A table comment is a metadata description that explains the purpose of a table. It helps developers and database administrators understand the role of each table at a glance, which is especially valuable in large databases with hundreds of tables. Comments are stored in the database catalog and are visible in most database management tools.

syntax.postgresql:
-- Add a comment to a table
COMMENT ON TABLE users IS 'Stores registered user accounts and their profile information';

sqlite.notes: SQLite does not support table comments natively.

tips: Add a table comment to every table describing its purpose and the type of data it stores.
Include business context in comments (e.g., 'Stores user orders placed through the web checkout flow').
Table comments are great for onboarding new team members who need to understand the database schema quickly.

name: Default Value
summary: A value automatically assigned to a column when no value is provided during INSERT.
description: A default value is a fallback value that the database uses when you insert a row without specifying a value for that column. Defaults help ensure data consistency and reduce the amount of data you need to provide in every INSERT statement. Common defaults include timestamps (NOW()), boolean flags (TRUE/FALSE), and status strings ('active').

syntax.postgresql:
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'pending',
  is_paid BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  order_number UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
);
syntax.mysql:
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'pending',
  is_paid TINYINT(1) DEFAULT 0,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  order_number CHAR(36) DEFAULT (UUID())
);
syntax.mariadb:
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
  status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'pending',
  is_paid TINYINT(1) DEFAULT 0,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
  order_number CHAR(36) DEFAULT (UUID())
);

tips: Use DEFAULT for timestamps so you don't have to pass the current time from your application code.
Combine DEFAULT with NOT NULL to ensure a column always has a meaningful value.
In MySQL 8.0+, you can use expressions as defaults (e.g., UUID()), but wrap them in parentheses.

name: JSON Column
summary: A column that stores structured JSON data inside a relational table.
description: A JSON column lets you store semi-structured data (objects, arrays) inside a relational table. This is useful when you have flexible or nested data that doesn't fit neatly into traditional columns, such as user preferences, API responses, or metadata. Most modern databases can query, index, and validate JSON data efficiently.

postgresql.notes: PostgreSQL offers both JSON (text-based) and JSONB (binary, faster queries, indexable). Prefer JSONB.

mariadb.notes: MariaDB stores JSON as a LONGTEXT alias with validation. Some advanced JSON functions differ from MySQL.

sqlite.notes: SQLite stores JSON as TEXT. JSON functions require the JSON1 extension (built-in since 3.38.0).

tips: In PostgreSQL, always use JSONB instead of JSON — it is faster to query and supports indexing with GIN indexes.
Don't store everything in JSON just because you can — if the data has a fixed structure, use regular columns for better performance.
Create GIN indexes on JSONB columns in PostgreSQL to speed up queries that filter by JSON keys or values.
